issue_title: Deleting DNS records that are associated with a fixed address
issue_body:
According to API doc, “ipv4address” search cannot be used by cloud api because this object belongs to Host Record creation.

> “ipv4address” object has restriction “The object cannot be managed on Cloud Platform members”

That is why host record worked but not fixedaddress.

Here is the API test:

curl -k1 -u cloud:cloud -H "content-type:application/json" -w "\nThe Response Code:%{http_code}\n" https://10.40.240.101/wapi/v2.3/ipv4address?ip_address=99.90.93.10
{ "Error": "AdmConProtoError: Operation read not allowed for ipv4address on Cloud Platform member in this version",
  "code": "Client.Ibap.Proto",
  "text": "Operation read not allowed for ipv4address on Cloud Platform member in this version"
}
The Response Code:400

But searching with fixedaddress works:

curl -k1 -u cloud:cloud -H "content-type:application/json" -w "\nThe Response Code:%{http_code}\n" https://10.40.240.101/wapi/v2.3/fixedaddress?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
[
    {
        "_ref": "fixedaddress/ZG5zLmZpeGVkX2FkZHJlc3MkOTkuOTAuOTMuMTAuMi4u:99.90.93.10/cloud_view",
        "ipv4addr": "99.90.93.10",
        "network_view": "cloud_view"
    }
]
The Response Code:200

So how come we did not see this issue before?
My guess is that NIOS had this bug and recently fixed the issue so permission is checked as mentioned in the document. However we saw that NIOS did not check superuser permission either.
We allowed the superuser on cloud api user but the API still failed. 

Anyway how can we resolve this issue now?

object_manager.delete_all_associated_objects() calls get_all_associated_objects().
This method does search using IPAddress.

obj.IPAddress.search(...)

If IP address is a fixed address, this will fail.
A funny thing is that delete_all_associated_objects() is only called from FixedAddressIPAllocator.

We probably need to make change in the interface from delete_all_associated_objects to
delete_associated_dns_records() and handle for DNS records associated the fixed address.

Also get_all_associated_objects is used by only delete_all_associated_objects.
So we need to change this method name to get_associated_dns_records.

Then how do we get DNS records from get_associated_dns_records?

Since we are not using admin credentails, we cannot use ip4addr and get dns records in one WAPI call. So we may have to do the following.

wapi/v2.3/record:a?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
wapi/v2.3/record:aaaa?ipv6addr=2001::1    <= if ipv6 is used
wapi/v2.3/record:ptr?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
wapi/v2.3/record:txt?ipv4addr=99.90.93.10
. . .
whateever record types defined in "dns_record_removable_types" to remove.

issue_title: Version 1.1.0's get_field() implementation is backwards incompatible

issue_body:
Hi! Thanks for doing all this work :-)

Now, I've been looking through the code a number of times, but I'm still fuzzy on the exact roles of `__init__`'s `fallback_on_wrong_date` and `fallback_on_missing_rate`. Extrapolating this issue, it might be beneficial to document all the arguments for at least `__init__` and `convert`, since the end user will need both to use this package.

If the maintainer is interested in receiving a PR, I wouldn't mind giving it a shot.

My understanding so far of `__init__`'s arguments:
- `currency_file`: A filesystem path (str or pathlib.Path), or an URL starting with "http[s]://"
- `fallback_on_wrong_date`: If False, convert will raise a RateNotFoundError when the requested date is outside our currency data's date range. If True, convert will instead use the youngest or oldest data point when the requested date is outside the range. It will still, however, raise a RateNotFoundError if you ask for a date that is missing inside the range, unless `fallback_on_missing_rate=True`.
- `fallback_on_missing_rate`: If True, the data loading process will fill in data for missing dates (between min and max date) with the closest rate. Otherwise, they will be left as None.
- `ref_currency`:  The currency that the source data is oriented towards. This is EUR for the ECB data.
- `na_values`: What to interpret as missing values from the source data.
- `verbose`: Standard flag for how much info it should print.

Is that about right? :-)

issue_title: Is there any efficient way to get execution type of testcase?
issue_body:
I would like to get all automated/manual cases for the test project. Currently, I am doing this in a very inefficient way.

```
EXECUTION_TYPES = {'manual': '1', 'automated': '2'}
project='test-project'
def getTestCasesByExecutionType(execType=''):
    testcases = []
    projID = tlapi.getTestProjectByName(project)['id']
    parent_ts = tlapi.getFirstLevelTestSuitesForTestProject(projID)[0]
    tcInfo = tlapi.getTestCasesForTestSuite(testsuiteid=parent_ts['id'],deep=True,details='full') #Have to call with details "full" as execution_type is only included in "full" details of testcase
    for each_tcInfo in tcInfo:
        if each_tcInfo['execution_type'] == EXECUTION_TYPES[execType.lower()]:
            testcases.append(each_tcInfo)
    return testcases

```
The above is not efficient solution as it has to get "full" details of each test case just to get the execution_type which takes a lot of time when number of testcases are in thousands. I would be grateful if you could point out any efficient method or other solution to determine execution_type.

issue_title: Compond fields should not be ModelField
issue_body:
DRF serializer allows us to create WritableFields for custom parsing/serializing/validation.
Fields lists and dicts should not be exceptions.
Proper implementation of list and dict fields is at: https://github.com/estebistec/drf-compound-fields/

Example:
```python
class FooSerializer(rest_framework_mongoengine.serializers.MongoEngineModelSerializer):
  class Meta:
    model = Foo
  phones = compfields.ListField(source='phones', item_field=PhoneField())

class PhoneField(rest_framework.fields.CharField):
  def from_native(self, value): 
    # strip non digits
  def validate(self, value):
    # count digits

N.B. 
In DRF3 WritableField is removed, and they have their own ListField.
They also recomend to remove any db-model-level validation and perform it on serializer level.
u"<class 'mongoengine.fields.BinaryField'> is not supported, yet

issue_title: Add ability for OAuth2 class to auto-revoke tokens
issue_body:
In certain use cases (e.g. not using `RemoteManagedOAuth2`, not using `store_tokens`), the client might not have a need for the access tokens / refresh tokens. For maximum security, it would be best if such clients called `OAuth2.revoke()` at the end of their program, so that the tokens becomes useless, in case an adversary was able to get them.

But the client may not remember to call `revoke()` in all relevant locations, so it could be beneficial to allow the object to auto-revoke its tokens.

I propose two mechanisms for this:
- Add `__enter__` and `__exit__` methods to `OAuth2`, making into a context manager that can be used in `with` statements, which would auto-revoke the token at exit.
- Add an optional flag to the `OAuth2` constructor. When enabled, the `__del__` destructor would call `revoke()`.

These would both be optional (the object would continue to work without being used as a context manager, and the constructor flag would default to `False`). One, for backwards-compatability. Two, because neither of these mechanisms is guaranteed to work (e.g. the process might be forcibly terminated; the computer may be offline at the time of the `revoke()` call), so we'll leave it up to the developer to decide how best to handle revoking. Three, because there are probably situations where revoking at exit is not desired, so we don't want to do that by default.

As pointed out @niphlod on https://github.com/web2py/web2py/issues/667, we can use the better `cursor.lastrowid`.

For more infos: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#lastrowid

Do not think it's very difficult to implement the use of domains in the definition of the fields.

A domain is a user-defined custom data type. It is used for defining the format and range of field

Now it can be implemented through a dictionary:

person_name = dict(
   type='string',
   length=100,
   requires=IS_NOT_EMPTY(),
   etc,
   etc
)
db.define_table('my_table',
    Field('name', **dict(person_name))
)

but the proposal is to make it more explicit:

person_name = Domain(
   type='string',
   length=100,
   requires=IS_NOT_EMPTY(),
   etc,
   etc
)

db.define_table('my_table',
    Field('name', domain=person_name)
)

db.define_table('other_table',
    Field('other_name', domain=person_name)
)


This is very comfortable when you want to change data types from many fields at once

issue_title: Update documentation in support of Cloudant Query support

issue_body:
Noticed in a log file that iterating over an `InfiniteFeed` object when calling the database method `infinite_changes(...)` had caused a stack overflow, small snippet from the stack trace:

```
…snip...
  File "/opt/sapi/manifold/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cloudant/feed.py", line 259, in next
    return super(InfiniteFeed, self).next()
  File "/opt/sapi/manifold/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cloudant/feed.py", line 164, in next
    return self.next()
  File "/opt/sapi/manifold/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cloudant/feed.py", line 259, in next
    return super(InfiniteFeed, self).next()
  File "/opt/sapi/manifold/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cloudant/feed.py", line 164, in next
…snip...
```
Appears that due to the `next` function being recursive on a skipped line (i.e. heartbeat, etc...) having several hundred skips in a row will blow the stack. This also can create a messy stack trace if there are a number of skips and an error.

Replacing the recursive call with a loop should resolve the problem, I'll follow this up with a patch.

issue_title: Support IAM authentication in replication documents.
issue_body:
_Parent Issue: https://github.com/cloudant/cloudant-sync/issues/31_

Currently `db.creds['basic_auth']` returns `None` when using an IAM client . As a result, all replication documents created using IAM clients _don't_ contain source/target database credentials.

_Work Required:_
* Expose the IAM key via the client session ([here](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/2.7.0/src/cloudant/_client_session.py#L194)).
* Rework the [Replicator](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/2.7.0/src/cloudant/replicator.py#L77-L93) class to correctly inject the IAM key into the replication document (when databases are backed by an IAM client).
* Enable replicator tests for IAM clients (currently disabled).
* Add more unit tests as appropriate.

# path to lapack library in case of a non-standard installation
USE_LAPACK_PATH =

# by default, disable lapack when using MKL
# switch on when there is a full installation of MKL available (not just MKL2017/MKL_ML)
ifeq ($(USE_BLAS), mkl)
USE_LAPACK = 0
endif

# add path to intel library, you may need it for MKL, if you did not add the path
# to environment variable
USE_INTEL_PATH = NONE

# If use MKL only for BLAS, choose static link automatically to allow python wrapper
ifeq ($(USE_MKL2017), 0)
ifeq ($(USE_BLAS), mkl)
USE_STATIC_MKL = 1
endif
else
USE_STATIC_MKL = NONE
endif

#----------------------------
# Settings for power and arm arch
#----------------------------
ARCH := $(shell uname -a)
ifneq (,$(filter $(ARCH), armv6l armv7l powerpc64le ppc64le aarch64))
	USE_SSE=0
else
	USE_SSE=1
endif

#----------------------------
# distributed computing
#----------------------------

# whether or not to enable multi-machine supporting
USE_DIST_KVSTORE = 0

# whether or not allow to read and write HDFS directly. If yes, then hadoop is
# required
USE_HDFS = 0

# path to libjvm.so. required if USE_HDFS=1
LIBJVM=$(JAVA_HOME)/jre/lib/amd64/server

# whether or not allow to read and write AWS S3 directly. If yes, then
# libcurl4-openssl-dev is required, it can be installed on Ubuntu by
# sudo apt-get install -y libcurl4-openssl-dev
USE_S3 = 0

#----------------------------
# performance settings
#----------------------------
# Use operator tuning
USE_OPERATOR_TUNING = 1

# Use gperftools if found
USE_GPERFTOOLS = 1

# Use JEMalloc if found, and not using gperftools
USE_JEMALLOC = 1

#----------------------------
# additional operators
#----------------------------

# path to folders containing projects specific operators that you don't want to put in src/operators
EXTRA_OPERATORS =

#----------------------------
# other features
#----------------------------

# Create C++ interface package
USE_CPP_PACKAGE = 1

#----------------------------
# plugins
#----------------------------

# whether to use caffe integration. This requires installing caffe.
# You also need to add CAFFE_PATH/build/lib to your LD_LIBRARY_PATH
# CAFFE_PATH = $(HOME)/caffe
# MXNET_PLUGINS += plugin/caffe/caffe.mk

# whether to use torch integration. This requires installing torch.
# You also need to add TORCH_PATH/install/lib to your LD_LIBRARY_PATH
# TORCH_PATH = $(HOME)/torch
# MXNET_PLUGINS += plugin/torch/torch.mk

# WARPCTC_PATH = $(HOME)/warp-ctc
# MXNET_PLUGINS += plugin/warpctc/warpctc.mk

# whether to use sframe integration. This requires build sframe
# git@github.com:dato-code/SFrame.git
# SFRAME_PATH = $(HOME)/SFrame
# MXNET_PLUGINS += plugin/sframe/plugin.mk

#!/bin/bash

# Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one

# software distributed under the License is distributed on an
# "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY
# KIND, either express or implied.  See the License for the
# specific language governing permissions and limitations
# under the License.


MXNET_ROOT=$(cd "$(dirname $0)/../../.."; pwd)
CLASS_PATH=$MXNET_ROOT/scala-package/assembly/linux-x86_64-cpu/target/*:$MXNET_ROOT/scala-package/examples/target/*:$MXNET_ROOT/scala-package/examples/target/classes/lib/*

# which gpu card to use, -1 means cpu
GPU=$1

# the mnist data path
# you can get the mnist data using the script core/scripts/get_mnist_data.sh
MNIST_DATA_PATH=$2

# the path to save the generated results
OUTPUT_PATH=$3

java -Xmx4G -cp $CLASS_PATH \
	ml.dmlc.mxnetexamples.gan.GanMnist \
	--mnist-data-path $MNIST_DATA_PATH \
	--gpu $GPU \
	--output-path $OUTPUT_PATH
```

issue_title: Weight sharing in SequentialRNNCell throws AssertionError
issue_body:
Using SequentialRNNCell's params= constructor option always results in an AssertionError with GRUCell. This seems to stem from the fact that the RNNCell getter sets self._own_params=False, and most cells (LSTMCell, GRUCell) call the getter even if they were not given a params= argument in their respective constructors.

## Environment info
Operating System: OS X
MXNet version: 0.10.0
Python version and distribution: Python 3.6.0 :: Anaconda 4.3.1 (x86_64)

## Error Message:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "myParamsTest.py", line 8, in <module>
    seq2.add(cell2)
  File "/anaconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/mxnet/rnn/rnn_cell.py", line 733, in add
    "Either specify params for SequentialRNNCell " \
AssertionError: Either specify params for SequentialRNNCell or child cells, not both.

## Minimum reproducible example
import mxnet as mx

cell1 = mx.rnn.GRUCell(10)
seq1 = mx.rnn.SequentialRNNCell()
seq1.add(cell1)
cell2 = mx.rnn.GRUCell(10)
seq2 = mx.rnn.SequentialRNNCell(params=seq1.params)
seq2.add(cell2)

## Steps to reproduce
python myParamsTest.py

issue_title: findObject unable to find non-UID objects in some cases
issue_body:
* Plone 4.1.6
* plone.app.linkintegrity 1.4.5

I was able to trace a problem where a reference object was not created in the event handler plone.app.linkintegrity.modifiedArchetype, which ultimately causes link integrity checks to not work. The issue appears to be because the rewriterule in my Apache configs causes findObject to work with what is an inaccurate path.

My url is something like http://myurl.com/portals/maryland/page-two and the portal path is /maryland. Note the extra "/portals" component to the url path that does not correspond to any Zope object

Snippet of Apache config:

    RewriteRule ^/portals(/.*|$) http://myinternal_ur.com:9507/VirtualHostBase/https/%{HTTP_HOST}:443/VirtualHostRoot/_vh_portals/$1 [proxy,last]

When plone.app.linkintegrity.handlers.findObject is called, the value of "base" is something like "<ATDocument at /maryland/page-two>" and the value of "path" is "/portals/maryland/page-one". "portal_path" uses the Zope physical path though, and gets "/maryland/". So "path" does not startwith "portal_path" and so it will first try to traverse to "portals", which doesn't exist, and it will return None object.

My fix was to replace this line

    portal_path = '/'.join(obj.getPhysicalPath()) + '/'

with

    portal_path = urlsplit(obj.absolute_url()).path + '/'

I noted the version at the top of this issue, but as far as I can tell this line doesn't appear to have changed in the current version.

Starting with Openstack Mitaka support for the auth_plugin functionality was deprecated in python-novaclient (see: https://github.com/openstack/python-novaclient/blob/master/releasenotes/notes/remove-auth-system-b2cd247b8a312b72.yaml) and since the mitaka-eol release it has been removed. #14 saw this coming.

One option is to bring back the pinned dependency to version 3.3.3. This is the last version of python-novaclient with support for auth_plugin (albiet with the depedency warning) and should work fine.

A better solution would be to migrate rackspace_auth_openstack to a keystoneauth plugin, which is supported by python-novaclient. I will open an issue for this upstream and start investigating.

issue_title: Improvements to verify the token through the jwks
issue_body:
Currently, the only way to verify a token using the RS256 algorithm is to hard-code the signing key in a settings parameter.

I'm not really an expert here, but I have seen other implementations (not in Python) that take advantage of the (possible) JWKS endpoint of the identity provider. Documentation can be found here: https://auth0.com/docs/jwks.

I have a local branch of mozilla-django-oidc where it's working. Basically I verify whether a signing key or a JWKS endpoint is set, and in the latter case I try to match a key with the key id retrieved from the token and pass it on to jose to run the verification in place of the hard-coded one.
It works like a charm, except the fact that I have to decode the token first and to do that I have to use a private python-jose method (`jose.jws._load`) which I think is blatantly taken from pyjwt.

I have seen that a new branch has been made that uses the new josepy package, and it seems that decoding the token using that is no longer a private affair.

Can a modification like that be of any interest? It seems strange to me that I'm the only one needing  such a workflow.

issue_title: Non-matplotlib figure objects need more than savefig
issue_body:
I have been using pytest-mpl to test the [gmt-python](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python) library. We have a `Figure` object that implements `savefig` and the tests were working fine in Python 3.5 and 3.6 ([an example test script](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/blob/master/gmt/tests/test_pscoast.py)).

A few weeks ago, our tests for 3.5 started failing ([example error log](https://travis-ci.org/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/jobs/275310274#L1930)). I reproduced the failing tests locally using a 3.5 version of our [default conda environment](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/blob/master/environment.yml). I managed to track it down to `plt.close` not recognizing my `Figure` class as a valid figure object (see [the pyplot.close code](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/master/lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py#L646)). 

The real mystery is that the same test with the same matplotlib version (2.0.2) passes on 3.6. 

Is there any way that pytest-mpl could avoid the call to `plt.close(fig)`? Even just removing the `fig` argument to close the current figure would already solve the problem. I can resolve this by introducing a `int` attribute on my `Figure` but that is very ugly.

If there is not way around it, then it would be good to include in the docs the need to implement an `int` attribute to fool `plt.close`. 

Otherwise, I'm very impressed that this worked so flawlessly! Congratulations!

At some point, we added functionality to describe different lists of classes in the `--help-all` output and the config file. I think this might have been when frontend config could be passed to kernels, but we didn't want to show those options in help output.

We're not using this functionality now, as far as I can see, and it's a little bit of extra confusing indirection to follow. I'd suggest we get rid of it again and just use `classes`.

If the answer is "it's just like traits" that doesn't help me at all, I have no idea what that does either. (Or at least today I'm wearing the hat where I'm pretending not to. ;-)

This makes it impossible to selectively silence it's debugging output - for example the (IMO quite useless) ["Using default logger" message](https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/blob/master/traitlets/config/loader.py#L342).

To reproduce:

import IPython
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

```

issue_title: Expose Central Scheduler metrics for easy ingestion
issue_body:
It would be extremely helpful to be able to get some metrics from the Central Scheduler via an RPC call. Will the visualizer gives you a view of what has been happening recently, it only shows a fixed amount of data (depending on your `remove-delay` config setting). Being able to continuously collect metrics would enable us to track overall health of our pipelines and the central scheduler.

Our particular use case would be to pull those metrics with Prometheus on a regular basis (although personally I'm still trying to understand how Prometheus works). But a more general solution not tailored to a particular metric collection tool, would still be helpful.

I've started exploring this and I'm still getting to grips with exactly what state the central scheduler stores. So I don't know what the complete list of potential metrics might be, but for starters they could include things like:

- Number of workers connected (per host)
- Number of tasks in each state (pending, running, failed etc) (per host where applicable)
- Average runtime of completed tasks

issue_title: Assimp 3.1.1 fails to build on big-endian architecture
issue_body:
I'm trying to build assimp-3.1.1 on the redhat 7 ppc64 architecture.  I'm getting the following error:
```
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp: In instantiation of 'std::size_t Assimp::Copy(uint8_t*, T&) [with T = short unsigned int; std::size_t = long unsigned int; uint8_t = unsigned char]':
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:93:44:   required from here
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:85:21: error: lvalue required as unary '&' operand
   std::memcpy(data, &AI_BE(field), sizeof(field)); return sizeof(field);
                     ^
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp: In instantiation of 'std::size_t Assimp::Copy(uint8_t*, T&) [with T = unsigned int; std::size_t = long unsigned int; uint8_t = unsigned char]':
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:94:44:   required from here
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:85:21: error: lvalue required as unary '&' operand
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp: In instantiation of 'std::size_t Assimp::Copy(uint8_t*, T&) [with T = int; std::size_t = long unsigned int; uint8_t = unsigned char]':
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:108:42:   required from here
/builddir/build/BUILD/assimp-3.1.1/code/Bitmap.cpp:85:21: error: lvalue required as unary '&' operand
make[2]: *** [code/CMakeFiles/assimp.dir/Bitmap.cpp.o] Error 1
make[2]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
```

Full build log is [here](https://kojipkgs.fedoraproject.org//work/tasks/3674/10373674/build.log).

It looks like the source of this issue is the in-line use of the AI_BE(t) macro, which on big-endian architectures expands to ByteSwap::Swapped(t).  Trying to pass a reference to the expanded statement to memcpy fails with the above error.

I was able to work around the issue by modifying the Copy function in Bitmap.cpp to create a temporary variable that holds the result of AI_BE, and then passing the temporary variable to memcpy.

The really annoying one is `W291` trailing whitespace. I wouldn't want to disable this warning because it's actually useful (although you can highlight trailing whitespace anyway), but since the `lint` command runs on every change, then every time you enter a space, the warning appears. In `nvim` this means my signcolumn / gutter is constantly appearing and re-appearing, which makes the text area shift left and right a column.

I think the problem could be remedied with more logic [here](https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server/blob/develop/pyls/python_ls.py#L112#L119) instead of calling `self.lint` on every change. Maybe every line would be a better frequency?

I would be happy to implement this at some point.

issue_title: Decouple the UCERFControl and UCERFSource objects
issue_body:
Right now the UCERF calculators use a UCERFControl class which is somewhat tricky: it plays the role of a source, but it also plays the role of a collection of sources, when a branch_id is not specified. Originally there is a single UCERFControl object which unspecified `branch_id`; during the calculation the `branch_id` is specified and the UCERFControl takes the role of a source. The problem is that  if somebody forgets to specify in the right place the `branch_id` and to call `.build_idx_set()`, the wrong data is taken. This is not theoretical, since it caused a real bug: https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/2537. Therefore I propose to use a cleaner design.

On one side, there is a single UCERFControl object, associated to the input file, without the concept of `branch_id` and without the concept of `idx_set`, which cannot be used by the calculators. On the other side, from the UCERFControl one can generate a set of distinct UCERFSource object, one per branch, each one with its one independent `idx_set`. The design if functional: `branch_id` and `idx_set` are fixed at instantiation time in UCERFSource and never change. This is more robust and actually correct: I discovered the bug https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/2537 while implementing this cleaner design.

issue_title: Remove the .geojson export for the hazard curves/maps
issue_body: It is useless. The way we visualize the hazard curves now (for instance in the Colombia project) is to export an .npz which is then converted into geopackage format and visualized by QGIS server. `.geojson` is a lot less efficient. Nobody is using the .geojson exporters to my knowledge, so they should deprecated and then removed.

The issue is only visible in calculations with huge logic trees, which becomes dominated by the data transfer of the RlzsAssoc, not of the probability maps. This happens in the postprocessing of Canada
which has 13,122 realizations. The RlzsAssoc object has a size of 4.85 MB. If 3000 tasks are generated (and they cannot be less other we would run out of memory), the data transfer is 4.85 * 1280 = 14.2 GB which is more than the probability maps of 5.5 GB. The net effect is that the workers do not work because they are waiting for the data to be transferred.
The solution is to reduce the size of the transferred object or to read it directly from the workers.

issue_title: Produce a `bcr-stats` output for the BCR calculator
issue_body:
The benefit-cost ratio (BCR) calculator, if used with a non-trivial logic-tree currently, produces outputs for each of the individual branches realized in the calculation. These are saved in the datastore with the name `bcr-rlzs`. Unlike all of the other hazard and risk calculators however, mean and quantile results are currently not provided.

The mean calculated across all realized branches should be provided for all BCR calculations, and stored with the name `bcr-stats`, similar to the other risk calculators. For consistency with the other calculators, the calculation of the mean results can be skipped by specifying `mean_bcr_maps = false` in the job configuration file.

Users of the BCR calculator should also be able to request quantiles calculated across all realized branches, by using the parameter `quantile_bcr_maps` in the job configuration file to specify a set of quantiles (eg. `quantile_bcr_maps = 0.15, 0.50, 0.85`).

issue_title: Sqlalchemy issue when setting multiple relationships in the same call
issue_body:
I've been running into some issues over the last few months setting multiple relationships in the same API call.  Here's an example error message I received earlier today trying to set two relationships in the same call: 

`sqlalchemy.exc.IntegrityError: (raised as a result of Query-invoked autoflush; consider using a session.no_autoflush block if this flush is occurring prematurely) (psycopg2.IntegrityError) null value in column "conversation_id" violates not-null constraint`

The problem seems to be related to the `apply_relationships` method in the `SqlalchemyDataLayer`.  As it loops through the relationship fields it tries to fetch the `related_object` and immediately sets the relationship with `setattr(obj, key, related_obj)`.  Although this works for the first relationship, it fails with the above error when it tries to fetch the second `related_object`.

I am able to get around this problem with the following custom data layer that waits to set the relationships until all of them have been fetched.  I'm not sure if this is the "right" way to solve it, but I wanted to post it here in case anyone else finds it helpful or can shed light on a better way.

```
class ImprovedDataLayer(SqlalchemyDataLayer):

    def apply_relationships(self, data, obj):
        """Apply relationship provided by data to obj
        :param dict data: data provided by the client
        :param DeclarativeMeta obj: the sqlalchemy object to plug relationships to
        :return boolean: True if relationship have changed else False
        """
        relationships_to_apply = []
        relationship_fields = get_relationships(self.resource.schema)
        for key, value in data.items():
            if key in relationship_fields:
                related_model = getattr(obj.__class__, key).property.mapper.class_
                related_id_field = self.resource.schema._declared_fields[
                    relationship_fields[key]].id_field

                if isinstance(value, list):
                    related_objects = []

                    for identifier in value:
                        related_object = self.get_related_object(related_model,
                                                                 related_id_field,
                                                                 {'id': identifier})
                        related_objects.append(related_object)

                    # setattr(obj, key, related_objects)
                    relationships_to_apply.append({
                        'field': key,
                        'value': related_objects
                    })
                else:
                    related_object = None

                    if value is not None:
                        related_object = self.get_related_object(related_model,
                                                                 related_id_field,
                                                                 {'id': value})

                    # setattr(obj, key, related_object)
                    relationships_to_apply.append({
                        'field': key,
                        'value': related_object
                    })

        # Wait to set relationships until all have been fetched from the db
        for relationship in relationships_to_apply:
            setattr(obj, relationship['field'], relationship['value'])
```

Not sure what happened but I have noticed recently the docs have malformed links in them. Here are just a few examples (I am sure there are more) - http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/sqs.html#client ```
  • can_paginate()
    • ``` Notice: `href="ecr.html#can_paginate"` should be `href="sqs.html#SQS.Client.can_paginate"` - http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#client ```
  • describe_images()
    • ``` Notice: `href="ecr.html#describe_images"` should be `href="ec2.html#EC2.Client.describe_images"`

issue_title: Feedback wanted for positional args for boto3 calls
issue_body:
Right now, to make a boto3 API call for a client/resource, you specify kwargs that match the casing of the underlying service API:

```
s3 = boto3.client('s3')
s3.put_object(Bucket='mybucket', Key='mykey', ...)
```

There's an [alternative syntax proposed](https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/632) that will also accept a single positional arg consisting of a dictionary:

```
client = boto3.client('dynamodb')
client.describe_table({"TableName": "foo"})
```

While this is mostly a cosmetic change, it does have a few marginal benefits: a little faster, pep8 compliant (no more pylint warnings about param casing).

This is completely backwards compatible change, but it'd be great to get community feedback about whether anyone would prefer this alternate calling style before making this a part of boto3.

Please let us know any feedback you might have about this change.

My code:
```python

ec2 = boto3.resource('ec2')
instances = []


for status in ec2.meta.client.describe_instance_status()['InstanceStatuses']:
    instances.append(status['InstanceId'])

def filterInstances(instances):

{'ResponseMetadata': {'HTTPStatusCode': 200, 'RequestId': 'c41f8fca-0ec8-473b-86a3-37dc9b189763'}, u'Tags': [{u'ResourceType': 'instance', u'ResourceId': 'i-xxxxxxxx', u'Value': 'xx', u'Key': 'Category'}, {u'ResourceType': 'instance', u'ResourceId': 'i-xxxxxxxx', u'Value': 'QA', u'Key': 'xxxxxxx'}]}

## Description of the problem/suggestion:
When calling `modify_instance_attribute` to modify case-sensitive values, boto is lowercasing the value which can lead to failures.

import boto3

As the error reveals, boto has lowercased the security group value, but it should not have (Security Group names are case-sensitive).

Boto 3: 1.2.2-2
Botocore: 1.4.70-1~16.04.0

Hi boto team,

 I have experienced the following event while attempting to send a request using a 'bytearray' payload representing an image:
* A network event triggers request.reset_stream() - https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/endpoint.py#L183
* My input type is 'bytearray', and thus triggers an UnseekableStreamError
* The constructor of the UnseekableStreamError attempts to call string.format(), however since my payload is not a valid 'ascii' encoded string, this triggers a UnicodeDecodeException.

This issue is related to https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/1359, in that the problem would be avoided if bytearray types are omitted from the seek(). 

I'm not a python expert, but I think the solution is simply to convert the following line https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/exceptions.py#L302:

from

fmt = ('Need to rewind the stream {stream_object}, but stream '
           'is not seekable.')

to

           'is not seekable.')

issue_title: Api constructor won't allow mode in create_with_paypal_security_test.py
issue_body:
### General information

* SDK/Library version: 1.13.1
* Environment: Sandbox

### Issue description

The front page of this repository points out the importance of checking for a compliant version of TLS, and directs developers to https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/samples/payment/create_with_paypal_security_test.py to check this issue.

This script sets the mode to "security-test-sandbox", which config.py's \_\_endpoint_map\_\_ maps to "https://test-api.sandbox.paypal.com/". 

But the Api constructor contains the following check:
```python
  if self.mode != "live" and self.mode != "sandbox":
    raise exceptions.InvalidConfig("Configuration Mode Invalid", "Received: %s" % (self.mode), "Required: live or sandbox")
```
Thus, the sample code cannot work. A mode of "security-test-sandbox" is not allowed.

The following change seems to give the desired results:
```python
paypal.configure({
    "mode": "sandbox",
    "endpoint": paypal.config.__endpoint_map__["security-test-sandbox"],
    "client_id": "<CLIENT_ID>",
    "client_secret": "<CLIENT_SECRET>"
})
```
Alternatively, the constructor could allow a mode of "security-test-sandbox".

issue_title: remote update does not do anything with kwargs
issue_body: The kwargs are just dropped

Hi,

The output of git status is no longer in the format expected by python-git, in
particular the Repo.untracked_files property parses the git status output
expecting a:
```
# Untracked files:
```
while later versions of git droped the #, also there are some subtleties in
the management of the file names (avoid the use of replace and rstrip
removes spaces not only '\n').

Here is a patch that fixes the untracked_files property. There might be other parts affected by the change in the git output.
```
diff -Naru python-git/git/repo/base.py python-git.new/git/repo/base.py
--- python-git/git/repo/base.py	2011-07-05 21:50:02.000000000 +0200
+++ python-git.new/git/repo/base.py	2014-02-23 17:54:32.157547255 +0100
@@ -512,35 +512,33 @@
 				return True
 		# END untracked files
 		return False
-		
+
 	@property
 	def untracked_files(self):
 		"""
 		:return:
 			list(str,...)
-			
+
 			Files currently untracked as they have not been staged yet. Paths 
 			are relative to the current working directory of the git command.
-			
+
 		:note:
 			ignored files will not appear here, i.e. files mentioned in .gitignore"""
 		# make sure we get all files, no only untracked directores
-		proc = self.git.status(untracked_files=True, as_process=True)
-		stream = iter(proc.stdout)
+		proc = self.git.status(porcelain=True,
+			untracked_files=True,
+			as_process=True)
+		# Untracked files preffix in porcelain mode
+		preffix = "?? "
 		untracked_files = list()
-		for line in stream:
-			if not line.startswith("# Untracked files:"):
+		for line in proc.stdout:
+			if not line.startswith(preffix):
 				continue
-			# skip two lines
-			stream.next()
-			stream.next()
-			
-			for untracked_info in stream:
-				if not untracked_info.startswith("#\t"):
-					break
-				untracked_files.append(untracked_info.replace("#\t", "").rstrip())
-			# END for each utracked info line
-		# END for each line
+                        filename = line[len(preffix):].rstrip('\n')
+                        # Special characters are escaped
+                        if filename[0] == filename[-1] == '"':
+                            filename = filename[1:-1].decode('string_escape')
+			untracked_files.append(filename)
 		return untracked_files
 
 	@property
```

issue_title: org.openqa.selenium.firefox.NotConnectedException: Unable to connect to host 127.0.0.1 on port 7055 after 45000 ms
issue_body:
Firefox version : 10.0.3 ESR		
<robotframework.version>2.8.1</robotframework.version>
<robotframework.selenium2library.version>1.4.0.8</robotframework.selenium2library.version>

Expected result : Firefox open with URL populated in address bar.
Actual result : Firefox open, with populating url.

Robot script:
Library        Selenium2Library
Open Browser  http://google.com  firefox

This library currently has following dependencies:

    decorator >= 3.3.2
    selenium >= 2.32.0
    robotframework >= 2.6.0
    docutils >= 0.8.1

Three first are actually used by the library, but docutils is only used for compiling README.rest and other reStructuredText files to HTML by `doc/generate_readmes.py` and `doc/buildhtml.py`. This is just a developer/packager activity and thus users don't really need docutils for anything.

I would like to remove the docutils dependency to make it easier to document what dependencies are needed in manual installation (see #255). Avoiding an unnecessary download when using pip wouldn't obviously hurt either.

I'm not certain is compiling reST to HTML really needed in general. Users looking at source probably are fine with *.rst source files and in GitHub (and PyPI #254) these files are formatted automatically. If the compilation isn't needed anymore, then the need for docutils obviously goes away. If there is a use case for the compilation, I would still be more than willing to remove the docutils dependency from users. If we want to be helpful for the developer running the aforementioned compilation scripts, we can change them to import docutils like this:

    try:
        import docutils
    except ImportError:
        raise ImportError('Importing `docutils` failed. Install it with `pip install docutils`.')

issue_title: Incorrect warning during the dependency resolution
issue_body:
When trying to `pipenv install populus` I get the following warning towards the end

```
Warning: Your dependencies could not be resolved. You likely have a mismatch in your sub-dependencies.
  You can use $ pipenv install --skip-lock to bypass this mechanism, then run $ pipenv graph to inspect the situation.
Could not find a version that matches eth-keys<0.2.0,>=0.1.0b3
Tried: 0.1.0b1, 0.1.0b1, 0.1.0b2, 0.1.0b2, 0.1.0b3, 0.1.0b3, 0.1.0b4, 0.1.0b4, 0.2.0b1, 0.2.0b1, 0.2.0b2, 0.2.0b2, 0.2.0b3, 0.2.0b3
```

As instructed I did `pipenv install --skip-lock populus` followed by `pipenv graph`

The last command (`pipenv graph`) listed the following (among other details)

    eth-keys [required: >=0.1.0b3,<0.2.0, installed: 0.1.0b4]

So it seems the install did go correctly (since it installed correct version of `eth-keys`)

Then why the warning ?

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type : macOS Sierra
1. Python version: Python 3.6.2
1. Pipenv version: pipenv, version 11.0.2

##### Expected result

There should be no warning since it installed correct version.

##### Actual result
```
Warning: Your dependencies could not be resolved. You likely have a mismatch in your sub-dependencies.
  You can use $ pipenv install --skip-lock to bypass this mechanism, then run $ pipenv graph to inspect the situation.
Could not find a version that matches eth-keys<0.2.0,>=0.1.0b3
Tried: 0.1.0b1, 0.1.0b1, 0.1.0b2, 0.1.0b2, 0.1.0b3, 0.1.0b3, 0.1.0b4, 0.1.0b4, 0.2.0b1, 0.2.0b1, 0.2.0b2, 0.2.0b2, 0.2.0b3, 0.2.0b3
```
##### Steps to replicate

    pipenv install populus

Pipenv doesn't resolve and install all dependencies of packages in my Pipfile. They also don't appear in the lock file. Recreating the lockfile and starting with a new env doesn't help it either.

See output of pipenv.help in "Steps to reproduce".

------------

##### Expected result

All sub-dependencies are installed correctly.

##### Actual result

`pipenv graph` outputs many question marks. `pipenv run pip freeze` confirms that the dependency packages have not been installed.

issue_title: pipenv install --system --ignore-pipfile throws an error in an alpine linux container
issue_body:
When inside a alpine docker container:
```
 # pipenv install --verbose --system --ignore-pipfile
Creating a Pipfile for this project…
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/pipenv", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(cli())

    return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/vendor/click/core.py", line 697, in main
    rv = self.invoke(ctx)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/vendor/click/core.py", line 1066, in invoke
    return _process_result(sub_ctx.command.invoke(sub_ctx))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/vendor/click/core.py", line 895, in invoke
    return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/vendor/click/core.py", line 535, in invoke
    return callback(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/cli.py", line 366, in install
    selective_upgrade=selective_upgrade,
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/core.py", line 1761, in do_install
    skip_requirements=skip_requirements,
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/core.py", line 636, in ensure_project
    ensure_pipfile(validate=validate, skip_requirements=skip_requirements)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pipenv/core.py", line 289, in ensure_pipfile
    project.create_pipfile(python=python)

    'python_version': python_version(required_python)[: len('2.7')]
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable
```

From a brief look at the cod, it looks like:
https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/ea0abf4536959c18d9c2bbe421980677ed2ef317/pipenv/project.py#L548
should pass in the location of the python folder, not just the virtualenv folder (if it exists), as python is installed in `/usr/local/bin/python` in my environment.

The documentation of `treq.testing.RequestSequence` says to use `logger.error` to report failures asynchronously, but this doesn't actually work because `RequestSequence` calls that function with a string. [Trial only fails the test if the log message has an associated Failure](https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/9188), which is not the case here.

We should consider making this argument optional and making `RequestSequence` log an error directly, rather than forcing the caller to pass a callback.

I need to convert "almost plain" objects to YAML. By "almost plain" I mean they're just data containers as if they were created by literals, but with different names. E.g. output of the [protagonist](https://github.com/apiaryio/protagonist) library.

However, `js-yaml` fails on those with `unacceptabe kind of an object to dump [object Result]`. There could be an option to treat those as plain objects ([json2yaml](https://github.com/jeffsu/json2yaml) does this by default).

Workaround for this is to do

```
var yamlResult = yaml.safeDump(JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(result)));
```

...but that's not very efficient.

issue_title: Have Tuple.values be a namedtuple so fields can be accessed by name
issue_body:
This was brought up as part of our discussion of the rejected #120.  What we want to do is:

- [x]  Submit a PR to Storm that serializes  [`TopologyContext.componentToStreamToFields`](https://github.com/apache/storm/blob/master/storm-core/src/jvm/backtype/storm/task/TopologyContext.java#L61) and sends that along as part of Multi-Lang handshake.
- [ ]  Add a `_stream_fields` dictionary attribute to the  `Component` class that maps from streams to `namedtuple` types representing the names of fields/values in the tuple.  This should get created at handshake time based on the contents of `componentToStreamToFields`.
- [ ]  Modify `Component.read_tuple()` to set `Tuple.values` to be a `namedtuple` of the appropriate type for the current stream (by looking it up in `Component._stream_fields`.

This will allow users to get values out of their tuples by accessing values directly by name (`word = tup.values.word`), or by unpacking (`word, count = tup.values`).

Some datasource changes could need to change the port in addition to the server etc. Some changes are a bit more involved but could include a port change. For example migrating from AWS RDS Postgres to Redshift might mean changing port from 5432 to 5439. A change of database variant could require other changes of course - schema in the relation for example.

See http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43761841/astropy-table-with-source-positions-save-in-casa-region-format

cc @vilhelmp

The `PolygonPixelRegion` is initialized with a `vertices` input, which it appears are *absolute* positions.  That makes it a bit more difficult to move the region to different positions.  Instead, should the `vertices` define a shape relative to `(0, 0)` and then have an additional `position` (or `center`?) paraemter that defines the absolute position (i.e. a shift from `(0, 0)`)?

issue_title: Wavelet denoising fails on odd-sized dimension images
issue_body:
From @grlee77 https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/2461#issuecomment-273717628

If you truncate the odd-sized dimension from 451 to 450 this issue will not occur.  This is a known issue with odd-sized inputs with `dwtn/idwtn` (https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/issues/80)

The proper fix is probably for to have `denoise_wavelet` itself either:
- [ ] raise an error if any non-channels dimension of the input has odd size


@stsievert Would you like to fit that in a new PR?

issue_title: Add skimage.future package, mention it in the documentation, move RAG code into it.
issue_body:
This stems from a gitter discussion. The idea here would be to put in functionality for which we haven't quite nailed down the API, to enable fast iteration on it as more people use it. The functionality would be guaranteed to stay in skimage, but the API would not be expected to be stable from version to version and would not follow our standard deprecation paths.

The RAG code from @vighneshbirodkar fits this description: it's working and useful (and on master), but we have not extensively tested it in real-world scenarios, so we don't have a good idea of whether the API is suitable for all use cases.

1. Add an N-dimensional transform module that supports the rc dimension, extended to N dimensions.
2. Rebuild `skimage.transform` to use this module

/cc for comment @scikit-image/core

Hello
I've found some strange behavior of function `skimage.color.label2rgb` that I would consider as a bug. I usually use this function for coloring results of pixel-wise semantic segmentation methods. This usecase assumes that I have a predefined and fixed label-to-color correspondence and want to transform labels to colors according to this. And this is how `label2rgb` worked in 0.12.3. But in 0.13 if the function takes array of labels where some label 'k' is absent, it will produce result where label 'k+1' has the color of label 'k'.
Small code example for reproducing:
```import numpy as np
import skimage.color
labels = np.array([0, 2])
colors = np.array([[1,0,0], [0,1,0], [0,0,1]], dtype=np.float32)
print skimage.color.label2rgb(labels, colors=colors)
```
It will print out `[[1,0,0], [0,1,0]]` as the result in 0.13, and `[[1,0,0], [0,0,1]]` in 0.12.3. The last one is what I would expect to get in this case.
Could you please check if it is an expected behavior or it is a bug?

issue_title: Change encoder output type to UInt[]
issue_body:
Encoders write output to `Real32[]` - it's a waste and has to be converted everywhere

As a part of https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/issues/961

EDIT: relevant discussion from the above PR

> The network API uses floats (Real32). The _C++ encoders are just the internals of network API_ Sensors, refactored out of the Sensor. So the encoders should use floats, right? (mrclws)

This is one I feel strongly about. And I think we found the cause of different views: "C++ encoders are just the internals of network API" - No. In my POV the hierarchy from more-core to less-core nupic.core is: {{ {Encoders {SP/TP/TM}} Anomaly/Classifier} Regions/Network} Swarming...}

See #948 , maybe the The network API uses ~~floats (Real32)~~ could use UInts/or some flexible SDR representation object? 

In either case, SP+TM use `UInt[]` as I/O, so you can't avoid the Real->UInt change inside those regions, and having Encoder (Real) only helps you in 1 region but forces others to convert when they want to use it directly ( #889 ).  (breznak)

issue_title: 2 step compilation for max raw performance after swarming
issue_body:
[Eigen used for small fixed sized matrices](http://eigen.tuxfamily.org/index.php?title=Versatility#Eigen_supports_matrices_of_very_different_sizes) but typically any `compiler!` doing optimization will perform a better job in optimizing: 
* loop unrolling
* reduced dynamic calls 

when data structures can be fixed-sized at compile time. To do this, we can run swarming to find out optimal numbers (# synapses per segment, # segments, # cells per col.,...) and then perform one more "production" build that:
* defines a flag to indicate the special build `-DFINAL_BUILD`
* uses `#define ...` to read constants for given structures from swarmed config file

issue_title: views: problem on recognize the content type
issue_body:
**Problem**
Today I found an issue when I was [saving a deposit](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/views.py#L493).
For some reason, firefox modified the `Content-type` from simply `application/json` to `application/json; charset=UTF-8`.
And the server wrongly interpreted it and returned error `415`.
With @slint we checked on `werkzeug` and found that the value inside `request.content_type` is the raw value and the correct one should be [request.mimetype](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/werkzeug/wrappers.py#L1717):

> Like content_type, but without parameters (eg, without charset, type etc.) and always lowercase. For example if the content type is `text/HTML; charset=utf-8` the mimetype would be `text/html`.

See [here](http://werkzeug.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/wrappers/#werkzeug.wrappers.CommonRequestDescriptorsMixin.mimetype).

**Solution**
Replace [this line](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/views.py#L493):
```python
if request.content_type not in self.loaders:
```
With this:

```python
if request.mimetype not in self.loaders:
```
------------------------------------------------------------------
cc @jirikuncar @lnielsen

issue_title: Supplementary unicode characters are mangled or throw UnicodeDecodeError
issue_body:
The unicode string encode/decode in pyjnius doesn't properly account for JNI's use of "modified" UTF-8: http://docs.oracle.com/javase/1.5.0/docs/guide/jni/spec/types.html#wp16542 

This causes pyjnius to not properly handle supplementary unicode characters (i.e. four byte UTF-8 code points), which mangles string inputs to Java and throws UnicodeDecodeError when decoding Java string outputs. For example:

```python
from jnius import autoclass
Stack = autoclass('java.util.Stack')
String = autoclass('java.lang.String')

emoji = u'\U0001F602'

stack = Stack()
stack.push(emoji)
popped = stack.pop()
assert emoji == popped # FAIL

stack.push(String(emoji.encode('utf-8'), 'utf-8'))
popped = stack.pop() # (PY3) UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't 
                     # decode byte 0xed in position 0: invalid continuation byte
```

The easiest fix is probably to pass strings through JNI's UTF-16 API (GetStringChars) instead of the modified UTF-8 API (GetStringUTFChars).

On Android platform, if Window.softinput_mode is set to 'below_target', the window size is corrupted (Window content is shifted to top of 20 or 30 lines depending on device model). 
This occurs **even if the keyboard is not used**. 
This issue **not** occurs with others softinput_mode.
This issue seems related with issue #544.
This code runs nice on Linux platform and was **not** tested on Windows, OS X or iOS.
The same buggy behavior is encountered with both Kivy 1.9.0 and Kivy 1.9.1.
This code was tested on 2 different devices with Android 6.0 Marshmallow (HTC m8 and Nexus 7).
The tool chain used was buildozer 0.31 with the following parameters:
```
android.api = 14
android.minapi = 8
android.sdk = 21
android.ndk = 10e
```
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8

from kivy.uix.boxlayout import BoxLayout
from kivy.uix.label import Label
from kivy.app import App
from kivy.core.window import Window

class MyClass(BoxLayout):

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(MyClass, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        softinput_mode = 'below_target'
        Window.softinput_mode = softinput_mode

class MyApp(App):
    def build(self):
        text = """
        Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit,
        sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

        Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui
        officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum"""
        setattr(self, 'text', text)
        
        return MyClass()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    my_app = MyApp()
    my_app.run()
```

        orientation: 'vertical'
        canvas:
            Color:
                rgb: .5, .5, .5
            Rectangle:
                size: self.size
                pos: self.pos
        GridLayout:
            size_hint: (1, None)
            height: 200
            padding: 10
            rows: 1
            BoxLayout:
                canvas:
                    Color:
                        rgb: .2, .1, .5
                    Rectangle:
                        size: self.size
                        pos: self.pos
        Label:
            size_hint: (1,.1)
            text: app.text
            text_size: self.width - 20, self.height
            size: self.texture_size
            valign: 'top'
```

More an idea as an issue.
Every MetaBlock could decode standalone in a thread.

Now an issue.
The blocksize is stored in meta-block header. (see spec. 9.2.) But only the final uncompressed size of block. The compressed size length isn't stored. That is a problem. Because the next thread will not know the position to start parallel the decoding of next block.

Solution:
Include a store information about compression block size.

issue_title: Need a way to ignore all undefined flags
issue_body:
```
Currently, when gflags module sees an unrecognized flag, it throws an exception 
and give up processing.  Even if we catch the exception and ignore it, we end 
up with a half baked flag registry.

The option "undefok" does part of the work, but it works only on specifically 
whitelisted flags.  It would be great to have an option to have it ignore ALL 
unrecognized flags.

issue_title: remove __hash__ from mutable classes such as Graph
issue_body:
see discussion in #718 : `IOMemory` by default uses `hash(graph)` to hash its `identifier` to then create hashed quads... all of this happens in light of "hashing a graph" meaning that its triples are hashed in a cryptographically reliable way...

maybe we should just not define `__hash__` for mutable things such as Graph...

issue_title: Make it easier to produce great3-like simulations
issue_body:
One of the takeaways from the recent "Great4" planning meeting at Penn last week was that people would like it to be easier to produce simulations along the lines of what was done for Great3, and then to improve on that in various ways for sims that test blending, selection, etc.  

There are four principal improvements to the config structure that I think would go a long way towards this goal:
- [x] Incorporate some of the recent features to GalSim, most notably the `COSMOSCatalog` class, to simplify the config files that were used to generate the Great3 sims.  In particular, I think all the parameter calculations that we wrote to catalogs and then read in as `type : Catalog` entries can be done directly in the yaml file.  This would make it significantly easier for people to see what is actually being done for these and modify the scripts appropriately for their use case.  I propose cleaning these up, adding comments, and then putting them in the examples directory (or better, in a sub-directory examples/great3) as cgc.yaml, csc.yaml, etc. so people can more easily find useful examples for making these types of sims.
- [x] Make truth catalogs.  This is already an open issue #301.  Part of the reason for our decision to make the catalogs and then read the values from there was that the catalogs would serve as truth information.  If we can make the truth catalogs directly, then we wouldn't need this two-step process.  I have an idea about how to do that, but I'll comment further on that issue page.
- [x] Add a command-line option to split the work into multiple jobs.  We put some hacks into the config generation scripts to make several config files per branch to make it easier to run on multiple machines.  If you're starting with vsc.yaml, say, it's not super trivial to make the necessary changes to split it into multiple runs.  There are a number of values that would need to be adjusted correctly.  However, internal to the config processing, it wouldn't be that hard actually.  It can just skip the appropriate output files and increment the numbers appropriately.  So I propose adding an option along the lines of `galsim vsc.yaml -n 10 -j 1` to split the work into 10 jobs, where this would only do the work of job number 1.  This would then be easy to run on multiple machines.
- [x] Finally, I think it would be useful to be able to define templates for various parts of the config file, so it would be easier to do some standard kinds of galaxies or psfs or image types, etc.  I'm not completely settled on what the best API for this would be, but here is a possibility.  The file great3_constant_space_psf.yaml could have the `psf` part of the config for the constant-PSF space branches.  Then rather than duplicate that in csc.yaml, csv.yaml, rsc.yaml, rsv.yaml, msc.yaml, and msv.yaml, all 6 of these files could just do:
```
psf :
    template : great3_constant_space_psf
```
which would pull the `config['psf']` information from that file.  If a template defined several top-level items, then they could either be incorporated separately (maybe several but not all of the items defined in the template file)
```
gal:
    template : cgc
psf:
    template : cgc
image:
    template : cgc
output:
    type : NumberedFile
    ...  # Maybe a different output structure here than what is in cgc.yaml
```
or all at once, which would get everything:
```
template : cgc
```
And then (especially in the latter example) it would make sense to allow for changes to the template info.  So the template would be a starting point, but any other items in the dict would supersede the values in the template:
```
psf : 
    template : cgc
    items.1.defocus : { type : RandomGaussian, sigma : 0.13 }  # Not so out of focus!
```
or
```
template : cgc
psf :
    items.0.fwhm : { type : Random, min : 0.75, max : 1.05 }  # DES has larger seeing than cgc.
    items.1 :
        type : OpticalPSF
        ... # Build a DES-like optical PSF
image : 
    pixel_scale : 0.26   # DES pixel scale
output.file_name.format : deslike_image-%03d-%1d.fits
```

I don't think the implementation of any of these changes will be terribly hard.  And IMO, the result would be a lot more user-friendly than trying to parse the great3-public scripts, which are a bit obtuse, to be honest.

issue_title: Feature request: Allow Custom Icon Layers to be Named and Added to the Library.
issue_body:
### Layers are very powerful yet not easily reusable. When configuring the Fontawesome 5 library you must import and add the icons you wish to use; why not also have the ability to define custom icon layers that you would be able to name, add and use in the app like any other normal icon. With this enhancement I should be able to write somthing like this: ```javascript import fontawesome from '@fortawesome/fontawesome'; import faSpinner from '@fortawesome/fontawesome-pro-regular/faSpinner'; let faCustomLayer = fontawesome.layer((push) => { push(fontawesome.icon(faSpinner)) push(fontawesome.text('Wait…', { transform: { size: 4 } } )) }) debugger; fontawesome.library.addLayer({iconName: 'wait', icon: faCustomLayer}); // or something similar ``` And be able to get the saved layer like this: ```javascript let faWaitLayer = fontawesome.icon({ prefix: 'fa-layers', iconName: 'wait' }); ``` ### or in the HTML with: ```html ``` ### Saved layers should behave exactly like their native counterparts. Concider this example, using the vueJS binding. The developer here has mapped an array of actions to specific icon names and prefixes and is using a for loop binding to render their actions: ```html
  • {{ action.label }}
``` It would be difficult for this developer to use a icon layer for one of those actions if their designer required it. How would you suggest solving this problem the way the framework is currently written? By allowing Layers to be registered as "custom icons" that work like any other icon you would allow developers to use the same strategy to display icons and layers when necessary. Please consider doing this sooner rather than later.

issue_title: Font shorthand doesn't support rem unit | IE10 Bug
issue_body:
The rem unit is not supported in IE10 for the font shorthand.

**Bug report:**
https://connect.microsoft.com/IE/feedback/details/772679/ie10-not-recognizing-font-decloration-when-rem-is-used-as-font-size-unit-of-measure

**Code Snippet:**
` font: normal normal normal #{$fa-font-size-base}/#{$fa-line-height-base} FontAwesome; // shortening font declaration`
(scss/_mixins.scss) Line: 6

**Solution:**
The only work-around is to specify the font-size again after the font property, or not use the shorthand when using rem.

issue_title: Add support for multiple Etcd endpoints
issue_body:
Looking at the docs specified here, it appears we can only specify a single endpoint for the proxy or endpoint.
https://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ENVIRONMENT.html#environment

The limitation here is that if that specific endpoint is not available on start-up, it's obviously not going to be able to auto-discover the other members.  Seems we could avoid this issue by allowing users to manually specify each member endpoint or each proxy endpoint they have available.  Patroni would then cycle through the endpoints until it is able to establish a connection.

  - apiGroups:
      - ""
    resources:
      - configmaps
      - pods
      - secrets
      - namespaces
    verbs:
      - get
      - list
  - apiGroups:
      - ""
    resources:
      - configmaps
    verbs:
      - create
  - apiGroups:
      - ""
    resources:
      - endpoints
    verbs:

issue_title: Does Patroni support Consul ACL via tokens?
issue_body:
Hi,

I couldn't find a mailing list or an IRC channel so creating this issue, but it's more of a question.

We're in the process of rolling out security to our Core Consul cluster by enforcing strict ACL rules but I'm not sure how Patroni would deal with it.

Does Patroni provide an out-of-the-box support for dealing with consul ACLs via tokens? If not, what's the recommended way to deal with a scenario such as this?

We've been thinking about setting the authentication parameters (replication/superuser) on the bootstrap section for them to be propagated over the DCS instead of having to coordinate their distribution to all nodes.

However, it seems that the `authentication` key from the dynamic configuration is ignored explicitly here:
https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/config.py#L172

Is there any reason for that?

Tested environments:
* datacats CKAN, latest master (2.5.0a)
* source install CKAN, latest master (2.5.0a), upgraded since 2.3 master
* ckanext-harvest with a few harvest sources defined
* custom schema via ckanext-scheming (no "extra" fields allowed in dataset dict)

```
ckanapi dump datasets --all -p 6 -r http://SOURCE > datasets.jsonl
```
This will crash with a server error and broken pipe on the harvest sources, which are specialised datasets and collide with ckanext-scheming's well-defined, but rigid idea of dataset schemas.

Workarounds:
* delete harvest sources at source CKAN
* ckanapi dump chunks of datsets (with offset and max. parameters) omitting exactly only the harvest sources.

On a side note, some old datasets, which have been edited last before we switched to ckanext-scheming, still have "extra" fields, which will crash during `ckanapi load`. Had to manually remove the `extra` keys from those datasets to make ckanapi load them.

issue_title: Guake should store user preferences in standard locations
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

* Install Guake 0.8.8 on Ubuntu 17.04

What should happen:

* Guake stores preferences in `~/.local` and similar freedesktop/XDG locations

What happens instead:

* Guake generates `~/.gconf` and stores preferences there
* This contributes to cluttering `~` with non-standard dot-folders

This will probably be resolved when #863 is completed.

issue_title: "error: unconfigured build_depend" for Python package message dependenciess
issue_body:
Of course, this error message can be avoided by declaring only an ``<exec_depend>`` or ``<run_depend>``, but that is contrary to recommend usage:

 * http://docs.ros.org/api/catkin/html/howto/format2/python_module_dependencies.html
 * http://docs.ros.org/api/catkin/html/howto/format1/python_module_dependencies.html

The consensus of the ROS buildsystem SIG was that message packages should always have build and run or exec dependencies, even when not strictly necessary, as is the case for pure Python packages.

I don't feel really strongly about this, but I recently found myself adding unnecessary ``find_package()`` and ``catkin_package()`` CMakeLists.txt configuration commands to suppress that error, so I thought it worth mentioning.

I have no good idea how to tell message dependencies from others, and this error message clearly does much more good than harm, in general. Maybe if a package calls ``catkin_python_setup()`` and has no ``add_executable()`` or ``add_library()`` it can be assumed to be purely Python and hence not in need of CMake build dependency configuration.

If this issue does not seem worth the effort, go ahead and close it.

Many thanks for your work on catkin_lint! I continually reap significant benefits from it.

In france, some parameters are [not loaded the standard way](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/issues/818) because the current way of handling parameters doesn't scale . To use them the "proper" way, we would have to do this:

 :scream:

```py
code_insee = menage('code_insee', period)
taux = 
(code_insee == "75001") * p.tx_transport.code_75001 +
(code_insee == "75002") * p.tx_transport.code_75002 +
(code_insee == "75003") * p.tx_transport.code_75003 +
(code_insee == "75004") * p.tx_transport.code_75004 +
(code_insee == "75005") * p.tx_transport.code_75005 +
...
```

This can be solved allowing to dynamically request the parameters. For instance:

```py
code_insee = menage('code_insee', period)
taux = p.tx_transport[code_insee]
```

Keep in mind than both `code_insee` and `taux` are vectors.

When calling delete_project on a project which is known to exist in a local jira instance, the API returns as if the project has been deleted but navigating to the projects page in jira reveals the project to still be there.

System:
* python: 2.7 running on Linux mint 17.1.
* python-jira: 0.47
* Jira: 6.4.4

Example test case:

   ```python
    class TestJiraClient(unittest.TestCase):
        jiraclient = JIRA(options={'server': 'http://jira.local'}, basic_auth=('jira_admin', 'mypassword'))
        ...
        def test_create_and_delete_project(self):
            project_id = ''.join(random.choice(string.ascii_uppercase) for _ in range(3))
            project = self.jiraclient.create_project(project_id, 'This is a test project with ID ' + project_id, 'jira_admin')
            assert project != False
            self.jiraclient.delete_project(project['projectKey'])
            with self.assertRaises(JIRAError):
                self.jiraclient.find_project(project['projectKey'])
   ```

Tracing through `jira/client.py` I get the following:

    http://jira.local/secure/admin/DeleteProject.jspa
    {u'confirm': u'true', u'pid': u'10202', u'Delete': u'Delete'}
    <Response [200]>

Printing the value of `r.text` reveals the response is coming back from the Administrator Access page.

The fix for this would be, if using basic_auth, to change the destination page to `/secure/admin/WebSudoAuthenticate.jspa`, set the payload to:

    {
        'webSudoPassword': self._session.auth[1],
        'confirm': 'true',
        'Delete': 'Delete',
        'pid': pid,
        'webSudoDestination': '/secure/admin/DeleteProject.jspa',
        'webSudoIsPost': 'true'
    }

then once this has succeeded, re-post the delete action.

```python
    def delete_project(self, pid):
        ...
        uri = '/secure/admin/DeleteProject.jspa'
        url = self._options['server'] + uri
        payload = {'pid': pid, 'Delete': 'Delete', 'confirm': 'true'}
        r = self._gain_sudo_session(payload, uri)
        if r.status_code != 200:
            return False
        ...

    def _gain_sudo_session(self, options, uri):
        url = self._options['server'] + '/secure/admin/WebSudoAuthenticate.jspa'
        payload = {
            'webSudoPassword': self._session.auth[1],
            'webSudoDestination': destination,
            'webSudoIsPost': 'true'
        }
        payload.update(options)

        return self._session.post(
            url,
            headers=CaseInsensitiveDict({
                'content-type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
            }),
            data=payload
        )

issue_title: Report the number of transport reactions with GPR vs the number of transport reactions without.
issue_body: As it is hard to identify the exact transport processes within a cell, transport reactions are often added purely for modeling purposes. Having a metric to highlight where assumptions have been made vs where there is proof could help direct the efforts to improve a metabolic model.

issue_title: Check for duplicate metabolites in the same compartment
issue_body: If a model is consistently annotated with references that allow us to uniquely identify chemical entities (only full-length InChI strings as far as I know), we can use this to identify duplicate metabolites i.e. where different IDs are used for the same chemical entity.

issue_title: datastore: very slow queries, single record by key
issue_body:
Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS
Python 3.6.3
google-cloud-datastore>=1.5.0

repro code:
```
from google.cloud import datastore
from time import time
start = time() * 1000
ds = datastore.Client('project-name')
key = ds.key('TestPerf', 'c10d6806-e3d4-4694-a8be-38f481817c')
entity = ds.get(key)
end = time() * 1000
print("duration : ", (end - start))

```

We are just starting out with google cloud platform and chose datastore as the option to store data because it seems the simplest, however we are noticing very slow performance on queries. Most common query is by primary key (above code) or `=` on one of the fields.

We are getting about ~200/300ms on the above query with a single entity or many entities in the DB. The queries are done either from a Compute Engine instance or Kubernetes clusters (both same perf).

We are not sure what to do from here, short of switching to a different DB option on the GCP platform. Any help would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!

issue_title: Document how to use Service Accounts with dev_appserver
issue_body:
It can be done via

```
$ dev_appserver.py \
> --appidentity_email_address ${EMAIL_ADDRESS} \
> --appidentity_private_key_path ${PATH_TO_KEY} \
> ${APPLICATION_DIR}
```

This will make implicit credentials "just work" on the dev appserver for GAE.

This is true of both bucket and blob.

It is a bit nuanced since the ACLs can be in both the [bucket resource](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets#resource) / [object resource](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#resource) but can also be ~~retried~~ retrieved directly via [`storage.objectAccessControls.get`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objectAccessControls), etc.

(ADDED by DJH): This is even more contrived since the ACL specific API [methods](https://developers.google.com/apis-explorer/#p/storage/v1/storage.objectAccessControls.get) also require an entity. (This especially frustrates @tseaver.)

As part of a datastore query, I get back entities that have a KeyProperty.  When trying to call a .id() on the KeyProperty, I get the following TypeError thrown:

TypeError: 'long' object is not callable

Upon examining the particular key, I see this (obfuscated):

<Key[{'kind': u'xxxx', 'id': 1234567890L}], project=xxxx>

Not sure exactly where the TypeError is being thrown, but it looks like the 'id' is being interpreted as a long somehow, when it's actually a standard integer.

I've had to workaround this by doing the following:
Key._path[0].get('id')

issue_title: Client library should not try to validate sample rate
issue_body:
The client library should allow validation of sample rate to occur on the server rather than trying to do it's own validation.  Allowing the server to check errors means that we don't need to update the client if the server expands the range of sample rates that it supports.

[https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/speech/google/cloud/speech/sample.py#L70][1]

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/speech/google/cloud/speech/sample.py#L70

issue_body:
I am using pathos for my project and it works nicely for multiprocessing. However for parallel python I've got a problem - the system hangs on job submission.  With some investigation I've found an easy way to reproduce the problem using standard example sum_primesX.py. with
